nativeRange.habitat: The High-Hat inhabits coral reefs, rubble substrates, sandy bottoms, and seagrass beds across the Western Atlantic and Caribbean, typically found at depths between 3 and 70 m in clear tropical waters.

diet.notes: Generally requires live foods initially — small crustaceans such as black worms, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, and amphipods are typically accepted first. May eventually adapt to frozen meaty foods; patience and target feeding are often advised. Has been known to forage sand for worms, small invertebrates, and occasionally very small fish when food is scarce.

avoidWith: fast-eating species that outcompete at feeding time

imageCandidates.notes: Pauline Walsh Jacobson; 960x720. Tack-sharp subadult, whole fish in frame, classic black-and-white striped drum with tall elongated dorsal fin, well lit and cleanly separated from reef background. Small corner watermark.
Pauline Walsh Jacobson; 960x720. Sharp juvenile side profile showing the dramatically elongated dorsal filament (the diagnostic 'high-hat'); adds life-stage variety. Small corner watermark.

Clinton & Charles Robertson; 800x533. Adult in seagrass habitat showing full body, black-and-white stripes and raised dorsal fin; different photographer/life stage for variety. Slight water haze in background.

summary: The High-Hat is a black-and-white striped drum with a dramatically elongated dorsal fin, generally considered a docile and shy species best kept in a well-established reef tank with ample hiding spots and live or frozen meaty foods.
careNotes:
The High-Hat (Pareques acuminatus) is a nocturnal species that tends to hide extensively during the day, becoming more active as tank lighting dims in the evening. Providing numerous caves and crevices among live rock is generally advised to help the fish feel secure; without adequate shelter it tends to remain stressed and may refuse to feed.

Feeding is often the greatest challenge with this species. The High-Hat has been reported to initially accept only live foods — small crustaceans such as black worms, brine shrimp, mysis, and amphipods are typically the first offerings taken. Transitioning to frozen or prepared meaty foods can require considerable patience, and target feeding with a pipette or turkey baster is commonly recommended to ensure the fish receives enough food before faster tankmates can intercept it. A well-established aquarium with a thriving population of microfauna (small organisms such as copepods and amphipods that colonize live rock over time) is considered beneficial for long-term success, as these provide supplemental foraging between feedings.

The High-Hat can be kept singly or as a pair; pairs introduced to the aquarium simultaneously have been known to coexist without conflict. It is generally considered peaceful with other fish but may prey on very small ornamental shrimp or tiny fish if given the opportunity, so it is best described as reef-safe with caution. Its slow, deliberate feeding style means it tends to lose out to faster-eating tankmates at mealtimes, and special attention to feeding is advisable in any mixed community.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, temperature, salinity, tank volume, native range, and diet. Tank volume recommendation of 700 L differs significantly from LiveAquaria's 70 gal / 265 L; average biased toward larger value used (500 L). Temperature max of 26°C consistent with LiveAquaria upper range.

additional.name: Fishy.Wiki
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 23 cm, temperature 22.2–25.6°C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025, kH 8–12, tank minimum 700 L. Consistent with reefapp.net on specialist parameters.

additional.name: Saltwater Aqua-Fish
additional.notes: Confirmed 23 cm adult size; temperature 23–26°C; pH 8.0–8.3; SG 1.020–1.025. Tank size 265 L (70 gal) stated, consistent with LiveAquaria. Temperament listed as semi-aggressive/territorial — diverges from peaceful consensus of other three sources; majority (peaceful) used.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy cross-check: order Acanthuriformes (Fishes of the World, 5th ed.) used; distribution confirmed Western Atlantic from North Carolina to Venezuela. Synonym Equetus acuminatus noted.
